1: ʾĂšer hāyâ dĕḇar-YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû hannāḇîʾ ʿal-haggôyim (ʾal-haggôyim): the superscription to the entire Oracles Against the Nations (OAN) collection (chs 46-51). The phrase ʿal-haggôyim (concerning the nations) positions these oracles as YHWH's sovereign word over powers beyond Israel — a universal claim of divine governance. Comparable OAN collections appear in Isaiah (13-23), Ezekiel (25-32), and Amos (1-2). The OAN genre is not mere political commentary but theo-political declaration: YHWH's mišpāṭ (judgment) extends to all nations, not only to Israel.
2: Lĕmiṣrāyim ʿal-ḥêl parʿōh Nĕḵô (Lĕmiṣrāyim ʿal-ḥêl parʿōh Nĕḵô): the historical anchor is precise — Pharaoh Neco II of Egypt met Nebuchadrezzar at Carchemish on the Euphrates in 605 BCE (the 4th year of Jehoiakim). Carchemish was the decisive battle that ended Egyptian hegemony over Canaan and established Babylonian supremacy. Jeremiah and Baruch were in Jerusalem during this battle (cf. ch36, where Jeremiah dictated the scroll in that same 4th year). The oracle thus interprets a contemporary geopolitical event through the lens of YHWH's sovereignty: Egypt's defeat at Carchemish was not merely Babylonian military superiority but YHWH's sentence on Egypt.
3: ʿIrĕkû māgēn ûṣinnâ ûgišû lammilḥāmâ (ʿIrĕkû māgēn ûṣinnâ): the imperatives are sardonic — the prophet issues the war-marshal's call to Egypt's army. māgēn (small round shield, for hand-to-hand combat) and ṣinnâ (large full-body shield, for archers and spear-bearers) represent the two main infantry shield types. The mock-military exhortation sets up the reversal of vv5-6: the very troops being rallied will flee in terror. This dramatic irony is a characteristic feature of OAN rhetoric.
4: ʾIsrû hassûsîm wĕʿălû happārāšîm wĕhityaṣṣĕḇû bĕḵôḇāʿîm (ʾIsrû hassûsîm): four imperatives continue the mock-rallying: harness the horses, mount the cavalry, stand in the helmets (kôḇaʿ), polish the spears (mĕraqqu hammĕrāmāḥîm, Piel of rāqaʿ — to beat thin, polish), and put on the coats of armor (liḇšû hassirĕyōnōt). The attention to military detail (shields, helmets, spears, armor) mimics the war-preparation genre found in Egyptian and Assyrian royal inscriptions. The full battle-array described in vv3-4 will be utterly routed in v5.
5: Maddûaʿ rāʾîtî hēmmâ ḥattîm nĕsôgîm ʾāḥôr (Maddûaʿ rāʾîtî hēmmâ ḥattîm nĕsôgîm ʾāḥôr): the rhetorical question captures the prophet's mock-astonishment at the reversal — just summoned to battle, Egypt's warriors are already (ḥattîm, struck down, from ḥātat, to be shattered/dismayed) and fleeing backward (nĕsôgîm ʾāḥôr). Mĕḡubbĕrêhem mukkaʾtîm ûmānôs nāsû wĕlōʾ hifnû (mĕḡubbĕrêhem mukkaʾtîm): their warriors are beaten down; they flee without looking back. Māgôr missāḇîḇ (Māgôr missāḇîḇ): "terror on every side" — Jeremiah's signature phrase (6:25; 20:3-4, 10; 49:29), which he applied to Judah and which his enemy Pashhur received as a personal name (20:3). Here YHWH extends the same terror-oracle to Egypt.
6: ʾAl-yānûs haqal wĕʾal-yimmāleṭ haggibbôr (ʾAl-yānûs haqal wĕʾal-yimmāleṭ haggibbôr): the swift (qal) cannot flee and the mighty (gibbôr) cannot escape — the two human strategies of survival (speed and strength) are both nullified. Ṣāpônâ ʿal-yad nĕhar-pĕrāt kāšĕlû wĕnāpālû (ṣāpônâ ʿal-yad nĕhar-pĕrāt kāšĕlû wĕnāpālû): they stumble and fall beside the Euphrates — the very river that defined Egypt's northern ambition. Carchemish was intended to be Egypt's eastward expansion; it became her burial ground.
7: Mî-zeh kayyĕʾōr yaʿăleh kayyĕʾōrîm yitgāʾăšû mêmāyw (Mî-zeh kayyĕʾōr yaʿăleh): the rhetorical question opens the second movement (vv7-12) with a description of Egypt as the Nile in flood. yĕʾōr is the Egyptian loanword for the Nile (used throughout Exod 7-8 for the Nile's waters); yitgāʾăšû (Hitpael of gāʾaš, to surge, rush) pictures the inundation waters in turbulent surge. The Nile's annual flood was the source of Egypt's agricultural life — Jeremiah uses it ironically: Egypt's rise against the nations is like the Nile flood, impressive but ultimately receding.
8: Miṣrāyim kayyĕʾōr yaʿăleh ûkayyĕʾōrîm yitgāʾăšû māyim (Miṣrāyim kayyĕʾōr yaʿăleh): Egypt's self-declaration is quoted: ʾaʿăleh ʾăkassel ʾāreṣ ʾôḇîdâ ʿîr wĕyōšĕḇê ḇāh — "I will rise, I will cover the earth; I will destroy city and inhabitants." The triple first-person imperfect (I will rise, I will cover, I will destroy) mimics the divine speech pattern of conquest (cf. Isa 14:13-14, where the king of Babylon uses ʾeʿĕleh / ʾāśîm / ʾĕdammeh in hubristic self-declaration). Egypt's pride matches Babylon's and receives the same judgment.
9: ʿĂlû hassûsîm wĕhitĕhôlĕlû hāreḵeḇ wĕyēṣĕʾû haggibbôrîm (ʿĂlû hassûsîm): the mock-rally continues with the international composition of Egypt's forces. Kûš ûFût tōpĕśê māgēn ûlûdîm tōpĕśê dōrĕḵê qāšet (Kûš ûFût ûlûdîm): Cush (Upper Nubia/Ethiopia), Put (Libya), and Lud (Lydia or North Africa — debated; cf. Ezek 27:10; 30:5). All three were mercenary forces in Egypt's army. The enumeration of Egypt's multinational mercenary forces is both impressive and ironic — their very diversity signals Egypt's dependence on hired power rather than the divine patron who alone can save.
10: Ûhayyôm hahûʾ laʾădōnāy YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt yôm nāqām lĕhinnaqqēm mîṣṣārāyw (Ûhayyôm hahûʾ laʾădōnāy YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt): the theological summit of the oracle — Carchemish is redefined as yôm nāqām (day of vengeance/retribution). YHWH as the avenger (nāqam) exercises his justice on his enemies through the Babylonian instrument. Wĕʾāḵĕlâ ḥereḇ wĕśāḇĕʿâ wĕrāwĕtâ middāmām (wĕʾāḵĕlâ ḥereḇ wĕśāḇĕʿâ wĕrāwĕtâ middāmām): the sword personified — eating (ʾāḵal), satisfied (śāḇēaʿ), and drinking its fill of blood (rāwâ) — is the combat-violence imagery also used in Isa 34:5-7 and Ezek 21. Kî zeḇaḥ laʾădōnāy YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt bĕʾereṣ ṣāpôn ʿal-nĕhar-pĕrāt (kî zeḇaḥ laʾădōnāy YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt): the battle is a zeḇaḥ (sacrificial feast) — YHWH as divine warrior hosting a sacrifice whose victims are Egypt's army. The same image appears in Isa 34:6 (YHWH's sword has a sacrifice in Bozrah) and Ezek 39:17-20 (the feast of YHWH's sacrifice).
11: ʿĂlî gilʿād ûqĕḥî ṣŏrî bĕtûlat bat-miṣrāyim (ʾĂlî gilʿād ûqĕḥî ṣŏrî): the command to "go up to Gilead and take balm" is mocking — Gilead's famous healing balsam (ṣŏrî, resin/balm; cf. 8:22; 51:8; Gen 37:25) cannot help Egypt. Lāšāwĕʾ hirbbêt rĕpūʾôt tĕʿālâ ʾên lāḵ (Lāšāwĕʾ hirbbêt rĕpūʾôt tĕʿālâ ʾên lāḵ): "in vain you heap up medicines; there is no healing for you." The phrase tĕʿālâ ʾên lāḵ (no healing/restoration for you) was used for Jerusalem in 8:22 and for Babylon in 51:8 — the same incurability that characterized unfaithful Zion now belongs to Egypt. The wound of military defeat is beyond pharmaceutical remedy because it is YHWH's judgment.
12: Šāmĕʿû gôyim qĕlônēḵ ûṣiwwāḥătēḵ māĺĕʾâ hāʾāreṣ (Šāmĕʿû gôyim qĕlônēḵ): the nations have heard Egypt's shame (qālôn). Kî-gibbôr bĕgibbôr kāšāl gam-šĕnêhem yaḥdāw nāpālû (Kî-gibbôr bĕgibbôr kāšāl): the collapse is total — warrior stumbled over warrior, and together they fell. The image of crowded, chaotic retreat captures the scene at Carchemish: the disciplined formation of an advancing army becomes a tumbling mass of fleeing men. International shame — the nations heard the noise of the collapse — is the polar opposite of the military glory Egypt projected in vv7-9.
13: Haddāḇār ʾăšer-dibber YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû hannāḇîʾ lāḇôʾ Nĕḇûḵadrĕʾṣṣar melek-bāḇel lĕhakkôt ʾet-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim (lāḇôʾ Nĕḇûḵadrĕʾṣṣar... lĕhakkôt ʾet-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim): a second oracle heading (vv13-26) introduces the announcement of Nebuchadnezzar's future invasion of Egypt itself (this likely refers to 568/567 BCE, when Nebuchadnezzar did campaign in Egypt). The oracle extends beyond Carchemish to the comprehensive humiliation of Egypt on its own soil.
14: Haggîdû bĕmiṣrāyim wĕhašmîʿû bĕmigdōl ûhašmîʿû bĕnōp ûḇĕtaḥpanḥēs (bĕmigdōl ûḇĕnōp ûḇĕtaḥpanḥēs): the three locations — Migdol (NE border fortress), Noph/Memphis (capital of Lower Egypt), Tahpanhes (NE Delta fortress where Jeremiah was taken, ch43) — represent Egypt's northern defense system. ʾEmōr hiṯyaṣṣēḇ wahāḵēn lāḵ kî ʾāḵĕlâ ḥereḇ sĕḇîḇāyiḵ (kî ʾāḵĕlâ ḥereḇ sĕḇîḇāyiḵ): "the sword is eating all around you" — the devouring sword of v10 now reaches Egypt's own garrisons.
15: Maddûaʿ nusḥap ʾabbîrêḵ lōʾ ʿāmad kî YHWH hǝdāpô (YHWH hǝdāpô): "Why were your warriors swept away? They could not stand, because YHWH thrust them down." The hāḏap (to thrust, push down) attributes Egypt's military collapse directly to YHWH's action. Egypt's ʾabbîrîm (mighty ones, strong ones — used of horses in 8:16; Ps 22:13) are not felled by superior human arms but by the divine dāhap. The passive helplessness of Egypt before the divine push echoes the exodus reversal: Egypt's horses and riders were once the greatest military power; YHWH dispersed them then, and he does so again now.
16: Hirbâ kôšēl gam-nāpal ʾîš ʾel-rēʿēhû wayyōmĕrû qûmâ wĕnāšûḇâ ʾel-ʿammēnû wĕʾel-ʾereṣ môlādtēnû (ʾel-ʿammēnû wĕʾel-ʾereṣ môlādtēnû): the mercenaries — Cush, Put, and Lud of v9 — abandon Egypt, calling to one another to return to their homelands. Mippĕnê ḥereḇ hayyônâ (mippĕnê ḥereḇ hayyônâ): "away from the oppressing/crushing sword" — yônâ (Qal participle of yānâ, to oppress, crush) describes the sword as an oppressive force. The irony: Egypt who oppressed others is now the one causing flight by her own oppressive judgment-sword.
17: Qārĕʾû šām parʿōh melek-miṣrāyim šāʾôn heʿĕḇîr hammôʿēd (šāʾôn heʿĕḇîr hammôʿēd): the mocking epithet for Pharaoh — šāʾôn (noise, clamor) and heʿĕḇîr hammôʿēd (he let the appointed time/opportunity pass). The phrase creates a new throne-name for Pharaoh: "King of Egypt: Nothing but Noise who Missed his Moment." The mōʿēd (appointed time, opportunity) refers to the moment at which Egypt could have acted decisively — instead his vaunted military power was only noise. Isaiah 36:6 uses a similar demotion-image for Egypt as a broken reed.
18: Ḥay-ʾānî nĕʾum-hammelek YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt šĕmô kî kĕṯāḇôr bāhārîm yāḇôʾ ûḵḵarmel bayyām yāḇôʾ (kî kĕṯāḇôr bāhārîm... ûḵḵarmel bayyām): the divine oath formula (ḥay-ʾānî, "as I live") introduces the certainty of the invasion. Tabor (the conspicuous mountain of the Jezreel valley) and Carmel (the promontory jutting into the Mediterranean) — two of the most visually prominent landmarks in Canaan — serve as similes for Nebuchadnezzar's inevitable, unmistakable coming against Egypt. YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt šĕmô ("the LORD of hosts is his name") is the divine-warrior name formula that authenticates the oath.
19: Kĕlê gōlâ ʿăśî lāḵ yōšeḇet bat-miṣrāyim (Kĕlê gōlâ ʿăśî lāḵ): "Pack your bags for exile" — kĕlê gōlâ (exile-vessels, the gear of a deportee) is the same image used in Ezek 12:3-7 for Ezekiel's enacted prophecy of exile. Egypt's inhabitants must prepare for the same deportation she inflicted on others. Kî-nōp lĕšammâ tihyeh wĕnisrĕpâ mēʾên yôšēḇ (Kî-nōp lĕšammâ tihyeh): Memphis (nōp) will become desolation (šammâ) without inhabitant — the very vocabulary of judgment pronounced on Jerusalem (4:7; 9:11; 10:22; 25:11) is applied to Egypt's capital. The empire is no safer than the covenant city when YHWH judges.
20: ʿEglâ yĕpēh-piyyâ miṣrāyim (ʿEglâ yĕpēh-piyyâ miṣrāyim): "Egypt is a beautiful heifer" — the calf/heifer metaphor plays on multiple registers: Egypt's abundance (like a well-fed heifer), her fertility (the Nile-blessed land), and her vulnerability (an unsuspecting animal about to be slaughtered). Qereṣ mîṣṣāpôn bāʾ bāʾ (Qereṣ mîṣṣāpôn bāʾ bāʾ): "a horsefly/gadfly from the north has come, it has come" — the doubled bāʾ emphasizes the arrival's certainty. Qereṣ (horsefly, gadfly; only here in the OT) uses a startling bathos: the greatest empire of the age is undone not by a superior empire but by an insect (Nebuchadnezzar as horsefly). The same reductive irony appears in Isa 7:18 (YHWH whistling for flies from Egypt and bees from Assyria).
21: Gam-śĕḵîrêhā bĕqirbāh kĕʿeglê marbeq (Gam-śĕḵîrêhā bĕqirbāh kĕʿeglê marbeq): the mercenaries within Egypt are "like fattened calves" (ʿeglê marbeq — stall-fed cattle, Amos 6:4; Mal 4:2) — well-fed, comfortable, and useless for real defense. Kî-gam-hēmmâ pānû nāsû yaḥdāw lōʾ ʿāmādû kî yôm ʾêdām bāʾ ʿălêhem ʿēt pĕquddātām (kî yôm ʾêdām bāʾ ʿălêhem ʿēt pĕquddātām): "for the day of their calamity has come upon them, the time of their reckoning" — ʿēt pĕquddâ (time of visitation/reckoning) is YHWH's appointed moment of judgment, the same vocabulary used for Israel's exile (10:15; 11:23; 23:12; 50:27).
22: Qôlāh kaṇṇāḥāš yēlēḵ kî-ḇaḥayil yēlēḵû (Qôlāh kaṇṇāḥāš yēlēḵ): "her sound is like a hissing serpent as they advance" — a double image: Egypt retreating sounds like a hissing snake (the serpent symbolism is rich, as Egypt's royal symbol was the uraeus serpent nāḥāš); simultaneously the invaders advance like woodcutters with axes (bĕqarddummôt). The serpent-sound in retreat is a devastating reversal of the royal uraeus symbol of power. Wayyāḇōʾû lāh bĕqarddummôt kĕḥōṭĕḇê ʿēṣîm (kĕḥōṭĕḇê ʿēṣîm): the attackers are woodcutters — Egypt's army is treated as a mere forest to be logged.
23: Kārĕtû yĕʿārāh nĕʾum-YHWH kî lōʾ yiḥqēr kî rābû mēʾarbeh wĕʾên lāhem mispar (kî rābû mēʾarbeh wĕʾên lāhem mispar): they cut down her forest — "though it is dense" (kî lōʾ yiḥqēr, for it cannot be explored/fathomed) — because Babylon's army is more numerous than locusts (mēʾarbeh) and without number (wĕʾên lāhem mispar). The locust simile for a conquering army is a common ANE combat image (Joel 1-2; Judg 6:5; 7:12). Egypt's impenetrable forests offer no shelter because the attacking force is numerically overwhelming.
24: Hōḇîšâ bat-miṣrāyim nittĕnâ bĕyad ʿam ṣāpôn (Hōḇîšâ bat-miṣrāyim nittĕnâ bĕyad ʿam ṣāpôn): "the daughter of Egypt is shamed; she is handed over to a people from the north." The feminine personification (bat-miṣrāyim, daughter of Egypt) is the standard OAN form for the nation as a woman suffering defeat and shame (bōšet). The passive nittĕnâ (she is handed over, Niphal perfect) credits YHWH as the agent: Egypt is not merely conquered; she is given by YHWH into Babylon's hands.
25: ʾĀmar YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl hinnĕnî pōqēd ʾel-ʾāmôn minnōʾ wĕʿal-parʿōh wĕʿal-miṣrāyim wĕʿal-ʾĕlōhêhā wĕʿal-mĕlāḵêhā (hinnĕnî pōqēd ʾel-ʾāmôn minnōʾ): Amon of No — the chief deity of Thebes (Upper Egypt; nōʾ = Thebes, Ezek 30:14-15; Nah 3:8), whose full name was Amon-Ra. YHWH's judgment explicitly targets the divine patron of Egypt: wĕʿal-ʾĕlōhêhā (and her gods). The oracle is not merely political but theological: YHWH asserts his sovereignty over Egypt's divine pantheon. Cf. Exod 12:12: "against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments" — the exodus pattern is being recapitulated.
26: Ûnĕtattîm bĕyad mĕḇaqšê napšām ûḇĕyad Nĕḇûḵadrĕʾṣṣar melek-bāḇel ûḇĕyad-ʿăḇādāyw (ûḇĕyad mĕḇaqšê napšām): YHWH delivers them into the hands of "those who seek their lives" (mĕḇaqšê napšām) — the same phrase used for those who sought Jeremiah's life (11:21; 44:30). Wĕʾaḥărê-ḵēn tiššāḵēn kîmê-qedem nĕʾum-YHWH (Wĕʾaḥărê-ḵēn tiššāḵēn kîmê-qedem): "But afterward Egypt will be inhabited again as in the days of old." This restoration footnote (like Moab's in 48:47 and Ammon's in 49:6) prevents the OAN from reading as pure annihilation — YHWH's judgment is not the last word even for the nations. Ezekiel 29:13-14 provides a parallel: Egypt will be restored, though to a lower status.
27: Wĕʾattâ ʾal-tîrāʾ ʿaḇdî yaʿăqōḇ wĕʾal-tēḥat yiśrāʾēl (Wĕʾattâ ʾal-tîrāʾ ʿaḇdî yaʿăqōḇ): the oracle concludes with a remarkable turn to Jacob/Israel — vv27-28 are nearly identical to 30:10-11. After the oracle of doom against Egypt, the word to Israel is ʾal-tîrāʾ (do not fear) — YHWH's universal sovereignty over Egypt is precisely the ground of Israel's security. Kî hinnĕnî môšîʿăḵā mērāḥôq wĕʾet-zarʿăḵā mēʾereṣ šiḇyām (hinnĕnî môšîʿăḵā mērāḥôq): "I am going to save you from far away and your descendants from the land of their captivity" — the same YHWH who controls Nebuchadnezzar over Egypt is simultaneously the one who saves Jacob from exile. Wĕšāḇ yaʿăqōḇ wĕšāqaṭ wĕšaʿănan wĕʾên maḥărîd (wĕšāḇ... wĕšāqaṭ wĕšaʿănan): Jacob will return and be quiet (šāqaṭ), secure (šaʿănan), with no one causing trembling (maḥărîd, Hiphil participle of ḥārad) — a sequence of three šin-rooted words that pile up the completeness of restoration rest.
28: ʾAttâ ʾal-tîrāʾ ʿaḇdî yaʿăqōḇ nĕʾum-YHWH kî ʾittĕḵā ʾānî (kî ʾittĕḵā ʾānî): "for I am with you" — the divine presence formula (ʿimmānû / ʾittĕḵā) that recurs throughout the call narrative (1:8, 19) and the letters to the exiles (29:14) is the ultimate assurance. Kî ʾeʿĕśeh kālâ bĕḵol-haggôyim ʾăšer hiddaḥtîḵā šāmmâ wĕʾōtĕḵā lōʾ-ʾeʿĕśeh ḵālâ ûyissartîḵā lammišpāṭ wĕnaqqēh lōʾ ʾănaqkeḵā (kî ʾeʿĕśeh kālâ bĕḵol-haggôyim): "for I will make a complete end of all the nations to which I have driven you, but of you I will not make a complete end" — the word kālâ (complete end, annihilation) is applied to the nations but denied for Jacob. ûyissartîḵā lammišpāṭ wĕnaqqēh lōʾ ʾănaqkeḵā: "I will discipline you justly but will not leave you entirely unpunished" — the tension of grace and justice: YHWH will not pretend the sin did not happen (naqqēh lōʾ, will not leave entirely unpunished) but will not destroy Jacob either. The difference between Israel and the nations is not Israel's merit but YHWH's covenant commitment to Jacob.
